pattern: Building the target one prefix at a time turns word choice into a shortest-path problem: each usable word at a position is an edge to a later position weighted by its cost, and the cheapest way to reach the end is the answer.
hints: Think of positions 0..target.length as nodes; using word w at position i (when it matches target starting at i) is an edge from i to i+len(w) costing costs[i].
A left-to-right DP works: dp[i] = minimum cost to have already built target[:i], and from dp[i] you can relax dp[i+len(w)] for every word w that matches target starting at i.
If dp[target.length] is never reached, no combination of words reproduces target, so the answer is -1; otherwise it's dp[target.length].

edge_inputs.description: statement example with a clear cheapest combination
single character target matched by a single-character word
the same word appears twice with different costs, cheaper one should win
a longer word would overshoot the target and must not be used
many overlapping short words forcing a real shortest-path choice

stress.description: target built from a small vocabulary of pieces, plus unrelated decoy words
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 400))
    alphabet = 'abc'
    vocab = set()
    while len(vocab) < 8:
        length = rng.choice([1, 2, 3])
        vocab.add(''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length)))
    vocab = list(vocab)
    parts = []
    total = 0
    while total < n:
        w = rng.choice(vocab)
        parts.append(w)
        total += len(w)
    target = ''.join(parts)[:n]
    if not target:
        target = 'a'
    words = list(vocab)
    costs = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in words]
    extra = max(0, min(60, n // 5))
    for _ in range(extra):
        length = rng.choice([1, 2, 3])
        words.append(''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length)))
        costs.append(rng.randint(1, 50))
    return [target, words, costs]
stress.note: guarantees at least one valid construction while still including decoys
